classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'Negroes or Mulattoes' in relation to restrictions on the sale of liquor. This is a clear indicator of racial segregation and discriminatory practice, characteristic of Jim Crow laws. The restriction is tied to a specific day (Sabbath), but the racial classification is the primary driver for the law's discriminatory nature. Therefore, it is classified as Jim Crow with high confidence. The category is 'public_accommodations' as it regulates the sale of goods to specific groups.

classification.title: AN ACT requiringallRailroadCompaniesin thisCommonwealth topayforstockthey negligentlykillor damage on saidroads.
classification.summary: This act requires all railroad companies in Kentucky to pay full damages to the owners of horses and other livestock that they negligently or carelessly kill or damage with their cars or agents. The killing or damaging of livestock by railroad cars is considered prima facie evidence of the company's carelessness and negligence.

classification.title: AN ACT exempting certain Property from Taxation.
classification.summary: This act exempts college buildings and seminaries of learning, along with up to five acres of their real estate and all personal property, from taxation. It also exempts real estate up to five acres belonging to incorporated institutions of the Independent Order of Odd Fellows and incorporated Lodges of Free and Accepted Masons. The provisions also apply to hospitals, infirmaries, widows' and orphans' asylums, and foundling asylums.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns tax exemptions for educational and charitable institutions. It does not contain any provisions that discriminate based on race or any other protected characteristic, nor does it imply any racial segregation or differential treatment. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to change the time of holding the Court of Common Pleas in Warren County.
classification.summary: This act changes the dates for holding the Court of Common Pleas in Warren County, Kentucky. Instead of the previously fixed times, the court terms will now begin on the second Mondays in April and October each year and continue until the docket is finished. The court can adjourn from day to day or time to time as convenient. The act takes effect upon passage but does not suspend proceedings at the current term.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of the late Clerks, Sheriffs, late Jailers, and other civil officers of this Commonwealth having uncollected feebills.
classification.summary: This act grants late clerks, sheriffs, jailers, and other civil officers, or their personal representatives, an additional year from February 12, 1871, to collect uncollected fee bills. It also grants sheriffs and town marshals, or their representatives, an additional year from February 12, 1871, to collect uncollected taxes for which they have already accounted and paid. The act specifies that fee bills are distrainable and that officers are subject to penalties for issuing or collecting illegal fee bills. Sheriffs and their representatives, along with their bond securities, are held responsible for any injury caused by illegal seizures or proceedings under this act.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text, Chapter 79, concerns the extension of time for civil officers to collect uncollected fee bills and taxes. It outlines administrative and financial procedures for these officers and their representatives. The confidence is high because the content is clearly administrative and unrelated to racial segregation or discrimination.

classification.summary: This act establishes the county of Lee, defines its boundaries, and locates its county seat at the town of Proctor. It also divides the county into six justice districts, which will also serve as voting precincts, and appoints commissioners to lay off these districts and designate voting places. The commissioners are also tasked with appointing judges, a clerk, and a sheriff for the elections.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act makes it a misdemeanor to willfully cast the carcass of any dead animal (horse, mule, cow, hog, sheep, or other dead beast) into any water course within Kentucky, except the Ohio and Mississippi rivers, or into any perennial spring or pond. Penalties include fines, with increased fines for subsequent convictions.

classification.title: AN ACT to impose an additional tax of fifteen cents for the purpose of increasing the Common School Fund.
classification.summary: This act imposes an additional tax of fifteen cents per one hundred dollars of property value for the year 1870 and each succeeding year to increase the Common School Fund. Crucially, the tax is to be levied and collected from the property of white persons only. Furthermore, the revenue generated from this and previous acts for common school purposes shall be expended and appropriated for the education of white children exclusively. Any diversion of these funds for purposes other than the exclusive education of white children will cease the levying of this tax.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that the additional tax for the Common School Fund shall be levied and collected 'of the property of white persons only'. It further mandates that the revenue shall be expended and appropriated 'for the education of white children exclusively'. This direct racial distinction in taxation and fund allocation for education is a clear indicator of Jim Crow-era legislation designed to segregate and disadvantage non-white populations. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial language. The category is 'education' because the statute directly concerns the funding and allocation of funds for common schools. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the heading 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and 'CHAPTER 453'.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend "An act to tax Railroads and other Corporations, in aid of the Sinking Fund," approved 20th February, 1864.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning taxes on railroads and other corporations. Specifically, it modifies the licensing fees for foreign express, transportation, and fast freight line companies operating in Kentucky. Companies with lines extending 100 miles or less in the state would pay an annual tax of $500, while those with lines exceeding 100 miles would pay $1,000. The act also exempts these companies from additional local licenses or taxes from counties, towns, or cities, provided they pay the state tax. It also dispenses with the annual report of business statements, requiring instead a report of routes and distances at the time of license renewal. Penalties are outlined for failure to report or pay the tax.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the taxation and licensing of express and transportation companies. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly targets or discriminates against any racial group. The provisions are economic and regulatory in nature, applying to all companies of the specified type regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It clarifies that the commissioners of tax for Elliott County are responsible for assessing taxable property within the county, and repeals the requirement for Carter County commissioners to do so for the portion of Carter County that was incorporated into Elliott County. It also states that the sheriff of Elliott County will collect revenue from this portion, and repeals the requirement for the sheriff of Carter County to do so.

classification.title: AN ACT concerning the State Agency at Washington
classification.summary: This act assigns the responsibility of collecting war claims due to Kentucky from the United States to the Quarter-Master General. It also authorizes the Commissioners of the Sinking Fund to employ an agent on a contingent compensation basis to collect outstanding moneys owed to the Commonwealth from the U.S. Government, with the caveat that no compensation will be paid on sums already audited and allowed. The Commissioners are required to take a bond from the agent for faithful performance.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the collection of war claims and financial matters between the Commonwealth of Kentucky and the United States government. It does not contain any provisions related to racial segregation, discrimination, or the restriction of rights based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the content is clearly unrelated to racial matters. The racial indicator is 'none'.

classification.title: AN ACT concerning the Quarter-Master General
classification.summary: This act continues the office of the Quarter-Master General for two years from February 16, 1870, with the provision that the Governor may reduce the office to a peace footing if Kentucky's war claim is settled or significantly advanced. It also includes a separate act repealing a previous act changing the line between Taylor and Green counties, and an act for the incorporation and regulation of various insurance companies (except life insurance).
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains three distinct acts. The first act concerns the office of the Quarter-Master General and its duration, related to post-war claims. The second act repeals a previous act about county boundary lines. The third act deals with the incorporation of insurance companies. None of these acts mention race or have any apparent discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, they are not Jim Crow laws.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Louisville Insurance Company
classification.summary: This act details the requirements for organizing and managing an insurance company, including provisions for capital stock, subscriptions, director elections, and investment of funds. It specifies that capital can be invested in bonds and mortgages on unencumbered real estate within Kentucky, provided the property is worth 50% more than the loan amount, with an exception for insured buildings.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the organization and financial management of an insurance company. It outlines procedures for capital stock, subscriptions, director elections, and investment of funds. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend and reduce into one the several acts in relation to Insurance Companies.
classification.summary: This act outlines the requirements for insurance companies to be organized and to commence business in Kentucky. It details investment requirements for capital, examination procedures by the Commissioner of Insurance, and the types of insurance that can be offered, such as insuring houses, buildings, and other property against fire, and making insurance on goods and merchandise. It also specifies the process for filing certificates and obtaining authority to operate.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the regulatory framework for insurance companies in Kentucky in 1870. It covers aspects like capital requirements, investment guidelines, and the process for obtaining authorization to operate. There is no mention of race, racial segregation, or any discriminatory practices against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from a specific year (1870).

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of Insurance Companies
classification.summary: This act outlines the permissible purposes for which insurance companies can be organized and operate in Kentucky. It specifies four categories of insurance: transportation, health and personal accident, deposit and safekeeping, and livestock. The act also includes provisions regarding the election of directors, the appointment of officers, and limitations on risk exposure.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the organizational structure and operational scope of insurance companies in Kentucky. It specifies the types of insurance policies that can be issued and the governance of these companies, including the election of directors and officers. There is no mention of race, racial segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Kentucky Mutual Security Company
classification.summary: This act outlines the operational framework for the Kentucky Mutual Security Company, detailing provisions for its management, the issuance and transfer of stock, capital increases, and dividend distribution. It specifies that dividends can only be made from surplus profits and outlines how unearned premiums and uncollectible debts should be accounted for. The act also details the process for policy issuance and attestation.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the incorporation and operational regulations of an insurance company. It covers aspects such as company management, stock transfers, capital requirements, and dividend policies. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1870.

classification.title: An Act to amend and reduce into one the several acts in relation to Insurance Companies
classification.summary: This act outlines regulations for insurance companies, including provisions on dividend distribution, real estate ownership, and the handling of notes deposited with mutual insurance companies. It specifies conditions under which companies can hold real estate and mandates its sale within five years if not necessary for business operations. It also details the security function of notes deposited with mutual insurance companies.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details regulations for insurance companies concerning financial practices, real estate holdings, and the management of deposited notes. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The language and subject matter are purely regulatory and financial, pertaining to the operation of insurance businesses. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This statute outlines the regulations for mutual insurance companies in Kentucky, specifically concerning the determination of the amount of a member's note in addition to the cash premium, the process for settling losses, and the requirements for the title of mutual insurance companies on their policies. classification.title: Annual Statement to be Filed in the Office of the Bureau of Insurance
classification.summary: This statute mandates that insurance companies organized under Kentucky law must annually file a detailed financial statement with the Bureau of Insurance by January 10th or within one month thereafter. The statement must include information on the company's capital stock, assets (real estate, cash, loans, investments), and liabilities. It also specifies requirements for stock policies and prohibits companies from operating on both stock and mutual plans.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the regulation and financial reporting requirements for insurance companies in Kentucky. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly discriminates based on race, nor does it establish separate facilities or differential treatment for any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1870.

It details requirements for statements, penalties for non-compliance, the authority of the Insurance Commissioner to revise forms and extend filing times, and mandates a minimum paid-up capital of $150,000 for out-of-state companies. It also specifies procedures for service of process on agents of foreign insurance companies.

classification.summary: This statute outlines regulations for insurance companies operating in Kentucky. It details requirements for agents to publish the location of their companies in advertisements, penalties for companies failing to submit annual statements, and the powers of the Insurance Commissioner to examine the affairs of insurance companies. The Commissioner can appoint individuals to conduct investigations and publish the results if deemed in the public interest, especially if a company's assets fall below a certain threshold.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the regulation of insurance companies and their agents. It details administrative and financial oversight requirements for these companies. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of any Insurance Company
classification.summary: This statute outlines procedures for insurance companies to address deficiencies in their capital stock. It details how the Commissioner of Insurance can require companies to call upon stockholders to pay in the amount of any deficiency. If a stockholder refuses or neglects to pay, the company can return their original stock certificate and issue new ones based on the ascertained value of the company's funds. The company can also create and dispose of new stock to make up the original capital. Directors are held individually liable for additional losses on new risks taken after a deficiency has been reported and before it is rectified.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses financial regulations for insurance companies, specifically concerning capital stock and stockholder contributions. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1870'.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An act to regulate the insurance business in this Commonwealth," approved March 14, 1867, and to amend an act entitled "An act to amend an act entitled 'An act to regulate the insurance business in this Commonwealth,'" approved February 23, 1869.
classification.summary: This act details the procedures for the Auditor of Public Accounts to hold securities deposited by insurance companies (other than life insurance) for compliance with laws of other states. It outlines the rights of companies to income and exchange of securities, and specifies that these securities are held in trust for policyholders and creditors in the United States. It also establishes reciprocal requirements for insurance companies from other states operating in Kentucky, mandating that they make deposits and pay fees equivalent to what Kentucky companies face in those states. The act also covers the payment of examination expenses by examined companies.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of insurance companies and their financial dealings, particularly in relation to compliance with laws in other states. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is on financial regulation and interstate reciprocity for insurance businesses. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It specifies the minimum number of persons required to form a company, the scope of business (limited to life insurance, annuities, and endowments), provisions for companies from other states, and the authority for reinsurance. It also details the process for filing a declaration and charter with the Insurance Commissioner.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to regulate the business of life insurance" approved March 23, 1870.
classification.summary: This act amends previous legislation concerning life insurance companies in Kentucky. It outlines rules for corporate names, prohibits misleading names, mandates the use of the corporate name in policies, and specifies investment requirements for capital stock and accumulations. It also requires life insurance companies to deposit at least $100,000 in bonds or securities with the State Treasurer for the benefit of policyholders.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY
classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1870 requires every Life Insurance Company doing business in the state to file an annual statement with the Commissioner of Insurance detailing its financial condition, assets, liabilities, and policy information. The statement must be filed by January 10th each year or within sixty days thereafter, certified under oath by two principal officers or a majority of the directors. The statute then lists 25 specific items that must be included in this statement.

classification.title: An Act to amend Chapter 57 of the General Statutes, concerning Insurance Companies.
classification.summary: This act details the requirements for annual statements from insurance companies, including financial data, policy information, and business operations. It also grants the Insurance Commissioner authority to revise statement forms, extend filing deadlines under certain conditions, and report insolvent companies to the Attorney General for legal action. Penalties are established for failing to file or for submitting false statements.

classification.title: An Act to amend and reduce into one the several acts in relation to the Insurance Bureau.
classification.summary: This act outlines procedures for the Commissioner of the Insurance Bureau to take action against insurance companies that exceed their powers or fail to comply with legal rules. It details how the Commissioner can apply for injunctions to suspend or prohibit a company's business, and how companies can petition to close their concerns. The act also specifies cost and expense recovery for examinations and proceedings.

There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices that would classify it as a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from a specific year (1870), indicating it's an act passed during a legislative session.

classification.title: An Act to amend and reduce into one the several acts in relation to Insurance Companies
classification.summary: This act outlines regulations for insurance companies in Kentucky, including provisions for receivers, annual reporting to the Insurance Commissioner, and the payment of taxes on capital stock. It specifies that life insurance companies incorporated in the state are subject to the act's provisions unless inconsistent with their charters. It also details requirements for companies to obtain authority to commence business and mandates that presidents, treasurers, or secretaries report capital stock amounts and pay a tax on it. The act also exempts companies from city or town taxes for state, county, or town purposes in years they pay the specified state tax.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details regulations for insurance companies, including financial reporting, taxation, and operational requirements. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is solely on the business and regulatory aspects of insurance companies. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act concerning Life Insurance Companies
classification.summary: This act outlines regulations for life insurance companies in Kentucky. It covers the distribution of surplus funds for mutual insurance companies, requirements for maintaining adequate funds to cover liabilities, and provisions for policies intended for the benefit of married women and their children. Specifically, it details how policies expressed to be for the benefit of a married woman shall inure to her separate use and benefit, and that of her children, independently of her husband or his creditors.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses regulations for life insurance companies, including provisions for policies benefiting married women and their children. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act outlines regulations for life insurance policies, including provisions for beneficiaries and creditors. It also details the permissible purposes and holding periods for real estate owned by life insurance companies. Furthermore, it establishes requirements for out-of-state and foreign life insurance companies operating in Kentucky, including minimum capital requirements and the need for agents to be licensed.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An act to regulate foreign insurance companies," approved March 10, 1869.
classification.summary: This act outlines requirements for foreign life insurance companies to transact business in Kentucky. It mandates that these companies must file a certificate with the Insurance Commissioner showing a deposit of at least $100,000 in stocks and securities for the benefit of policyholders. It also requires companies to file a copy of their charter and other organic laws with the Auditor, and to obtain a license to operate. Agents' licenses must be renewed annually. State agents or managers are required to submit a sworn statement of premiums received and insurance effected twice a year.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of foreign life insurance companies operating within Kentucky. It details financial requirements, licensing procedures, and reporting obligations for these companies and their agents. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The statute appears to be purely economic and regulatory in nature, related to maritime commerce. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to provide for the establishment of public landings and wharves on the Ohio river, and other navigable streams in this Commonwealth,' approved March 10, 1865.
classification.summary: This statute outlines regulations for wharfage fees, the landing and securing of watercraft, and the maintenance of wharf facilities. It specifies how rates should be set based on boat capacity, the privilege of unloading, and daily charges. It also prohibits charging fees for simply mooring to the shore and sets penalties for violations. Additionally, it mandates the owner of a wharf to maintain a floating platform or lighter and a secure apron for loading and unloading goods.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the regulation of wharves and river traffic, including setting rates for wharfage and the conditions for landing boats. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The regulations appear to be purely economic and logistical in nature, applying to all users of the wharves. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It outlines requirements for warehouse proprietors, including record-keeping, fee structures for services like receiving, coopering, selling, and collecting payments. Proprietors are also required to post a bond to ensure they pay over proceeds to the planters or sellers.

It details operational requirements for warehouses, fee structures, and bonding for proprietors. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An act to regulate the inspection and sale of tobacco in the city of Louisville," approved March 10, 1869.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law regarding the inspection and sale of tobacco in Louisville. It details storage fees, the appointment and duties of tobacco inspectors and weighers, and the process for sampling, sealing, and stamping tobacco hogsheads. The Governor appoints inspectors from a list provided by warehousemen and has the power to remove them. Inspectors are paid per hogshead and must be in attendance during business hours. Weighers are also appointed by the Governor.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided details regulations for the inspection and sale of tobacco in Louisville, including the appointment and duties of inspectors and weighers. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is purely on the commercial regulation of tobacco. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1870, indicating it's an act passed during a legislative session.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to regulate the inspection and weighing of tobacco', approved March 10, 1870.
classification.summary: This act details the appointment, duties, compensation, and responsibilities of tobacco weighers in Louisville warehouses. It specifies how tobacco should be weighed, sampled, marked, and how tare weight should be taken. It also outlines the oath weighers must take, their rotation between warehouses, their fees, and the process for their removal and replacement. Additionally, it mandates that weighers procure standard weights to test scales and outlines their responsibility for errors in weighing. The act also states that tobacco sold at auction must be weighed and marked as specified, and that warehouse proprietors settle with sellers based on the initial weight.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text describes regulations for the weighing of tobacco in Louisville warehouses. It outlines the process for appointing weighers, their duties, compensation, and responsibilities. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be purely economic and regulatory in nature, focused on the tobacco trade. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Reduce into One the Several Acts in Relation to the Inspection and Sale of Tobacco in the City of Louisville
classification.summary: This act details regulations for the inspection and sale of tobacco in Louisville warehouses. It covers weighers' duties, appointment of temporary weighers, terms of office for inspectors and weighers, penalties for tampering with samples or weights, the right of planters to choose warehouses and refuse auction prices, warehouse liens, restrictions on inspectors and proprietors purchasing leaf tobacco, the assignability of warehouse notes, interest and exchange charges on advances, exemption from auction duties, and repeals previous acts on the subject. The act is to take effect from its passage and remain in force for four years.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of tobacco inspection and sales in Louisville. It outlines procedures, fees, and penalties related to the tobacco trade. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in 1870. The confidence is high because the content is clearly focused on commercial regulations and lacks any racial component.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act,entitled "An act to amend the law in relation to Executors,Administrators,and Curators," approved January 24th,1856.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning executors, administrators, and curators. It allows for the removal of a fiduciary if they become insolvent or are in failing circumstances, or if their securities become insolvent or are in failing circumstances. It also outlines procedures for requiring a new bond with good security and the consequences of failure to comply, including removal from trust. This applies when the fiduciary has taken the benefit of the bankrupt law of the United States and their securities are insolvent.

classification.summary: This act allows trustees or assignees in conveyances for the benefit of creditors to resign their trust. It outlines the process for resignation, including settling accounts, giving notice to the assignor and creditors, and appointing a successor. The resignation does not discharge the trustee or their sureties from any liability already incurred.

There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly create racial distinctions or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to provide for the organization and regulation of Incorporated Companies, except Banking, Insurance, and Railroad Companies.
classification.summary: This act outlines the process for forming incorporated companies in Kentucky, excluding banking, insurance, and railroad companies. It details the requirements for articles of incorporation, powers of corporations, and public notices. The act specifies that any number of persons can associate for lawful business, except for banking and insurance, and for the construction of railroads. It grants corporations powers such as perpetual succession, the ability to sue and be sued, and to make contracts. Corporations involved in internal improvements must file additional documentation with the Secretary of State.

classification.title: An Act to Provide for the Creation of Corporations
classification.summary: This act outlines the requirements for forming corporations in Kentucky, including the necessary information to be included in articles of incorporation, the process for commencing business, the duration of corporations, and rules for dissolution and public disclosure of corporate information. It specifies that corporations for internal improvements may endure for fifty years, while others are limited to twenty-five years, with provisions for renewal. It also mandates that by-laws and financial statements be posted publicly at the principal place of business.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal framework for establishing and operating corporations. It covers aspects such as the content of articles of incorporation, commencement of business, duration, dissolution, and public disclosure requirements. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: An Act to amend and reduce into one the several acts in relation to corporations
classification.summary: This act outlines provisions for the formation and operation of corporations in Kentucky. It details the process for compelling the production of corporate books as evidence, establishes presumptions of legal organization, and prevents the defense of lack of legal organization in lawsuits. It also specifies fees for clerks and the Secretary of State, restricts the construction of railways and other infrastructure by mining and manufacturing companies, repeals conflicting parts of a previous act, and mandates publication of the act.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text (Sections 22-29 of the Laws of Kentucky, 1870) deals with the legal and operational aspects of corporations. It covers topics such as the production of corporate records in legal proceedings, the legal standing of corporations, fees associated with incorporation, and restrictions on certain types of corporate infrastructure. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to repeal in part and amend an act, entitled "An act providing a general Mechanics' Lien Law for certain counties."
classification.summary: This act repeals section thirteen of an act from 1858 concerning mechanics' liens in certain counties and extends the provisions of that act and its 1865 amendment to all counties in the Commonwealth. It also repeals any other acts or parts of acts providing special mechanics' lien laws for certain cities and counties that are inconsistent with this act.

The text does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly refers to race, segregation, or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: AN ACT in relation to submitting questions of Taxation to a vote of the People
classification.summary: This act prohibits any county judge, county court, police judge, justice of the peace, or incorporated company from submitting more than one proposition for taxation, direct or indirect, to the voters of a county, city, or town at any one election. Any tax, subscription, or donation voted or granted under conditions violating this provision shall be null and void.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute, "AN ACT in relation to submitting questions of Taxation to a vote of the People," approved March 17, 1870, deals with the process of local taxation and voter propositions. It limits the number of tax-related questions that can be presented to voters at a single election. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly during a legislative session, as indicated by the 'Acts of 1870' header and the approval date.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to prevent the spread of the disease called Glanders in this Commonwealth," approved February 16th, 1866.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the disease glanders in animals. It makes it a high misdemeanor, punishable by a fine of up to five hundred dollars, for any person to sell, trade, or convey a diseased animal by gift or otherwise. However, it includes a proviso that no person will be held responsible, civilly or criminally, unless it is shown that they were aware the animal was diseased with glanders.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses animal health and disease prevention, specifically concerning glanders. There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply to all individuals regardless of their race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. Confidence is high because the content is clearly unrelated to racial segregation or discrimination.

classification.summary: This act appropriates various sums of money to individuals serving in the General Assembly of Kentucky, including speakers, clerks, sergeants-at-arms, door-keepers, enrolling committee clerks, pages, and ministers of the gospel in Frankfort. It also details per diem payments for specific individuals assisting the House of Representatives.

Neither section contains any language, explicit or implicit, that suggests racial discrimination or segregation. The focus is solely on legislative procedures and financial allocations. Therefore, this statute is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Appropriations for Legislative Expenses
classification.summary: This statute details various appropriations made by the Kentucky General Assembly in 1870 for services rendered and articles furnished to the Senate and House of Representatives. This includes payments to individuals for their roles as Sergeant-at-Arms, for waiting on the Senate, sawing wood, furniture, articles, work done, repairs, and for boarding a committee. It also includes appropriations for the principal clerks for enrolling bills and resolutions, and for assistant clerk hire, as well as for preparing indexes to the journals.

classification.summary: This statute details appropriations of funds to various publishers for newspapers and to individuals for expenses incurred by members of the General Assembly, including committee work and travel. It lists payments to publishers of the Columbia Spectator, Glasgow Times, Louisville Commercial, Flemingsburg Democrat, Owen News, Daily Yeoman, Frankfort Commonwealth, Courier-Journal, and Hickman Courier. It also includes payments for sundries furnished to the Senate and House of Representatives, washing towels, clerk services, foreign newspapers, hotel expenses for a tobacco committee member, expenses for a committee visiting an asylum, boarding for an insurance committee, and services rendered by the Speaker pro tem.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of Common Schools in Kentucky.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Superintendent of Public Instruction to certify and direct payment for common schools in specific districts. Payment is to be made from any surplus for the school year 1869, at a rate not exceeding previous payments. Conditions include that payment has not already been made, prorated payment for schools taught less than three months, and provisions for insufficient surplus funds. Reports must be received by May 20, 1870.
classification.reasoning: The statute's title and text clearly indicate it is an act concerning the funding and administration of common schools in Kentucky. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. The provisions relate to financial disbursement and reporting for educational institutions. The confidence is high because the text is explicit in its subject matter and lacks any racial indicators.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the common schools of Kentucky," approved March 23, 1860, and to repeal an act entitled "An Act to amend the common school law," approved March 19, 1869.
classification.summary: This act establishes "The Board of Education for the State of Kentucky" as a body politic and corporate, composed of the Attorney General, Secretary of State, and Superintendent of Public Instruction. The Board is empowered to hold and dispose of real and personal estate for the benefit of common schools, adopt regulations for school governance, recommend courses of instruction, and prescribe forms for reporting by county officials. The curriculum is to include the elements of a plain education in English, such as grammar, arithmetic, geography, and history, but does not prohibit the teaching of other languages or sciences.

It details the composition of the board, its powers regarding property and governance, and the prescribed curriculum. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: An Act to amend and reduce into one the several acts in relation to common schools.
classification.summary: This act defines what constitutes a common school, emphasizing that it must be accessible to all white children between the ages of six and twenty years, regardless of their ability to contribute to expenses. It also outlines requirements for school operation, teacher employment, and the mandatory attendance of apprenticed white children at common schools. Failure to comply with the apprenticeship attendance requirement results in a fine.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white child' in sections §1 and §6, indicating a racial distinction in the application of educational provisions. Section §1 states that a school is only considered a 'common school' if 'every white child residing in the district... has had the privilege of attending'. Section §6 mandates that 'any person to whom a white child may hereafter be apprenticed shall send such child... to a common school'. This explicit racial classification in the context of mandatory education and access to common schools strongly suggests a Jim Crow-era law designed to segregate or prioritize educational access based on race. The confidence is high due to the explicit mention of 'white child' in defining eligibility for common schools and in the context of mandatory attendance for apprentices. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' from the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 133 1870 .'.

classification.title: An Act to amend and reduce into one the several acts in relation to Public Schools
classification.summary: This section of the 1870 Kentucky Laws outlines regulations for common schools. Notably, Section 9 allows for the establishment of a common school district that includes a university, college, academy, or high school exclusively devoted to the education of white children. This institution's managers would act as common school trustees for the district, and white children residing in the district would have free access to the institution for at least five months a year. This explicitly creates a separate educational system for white children, a hallmark of Jim Crow segregation.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white children' in Section 9, stating that a college, academy, or high school 'exclusively devoted to the education of white children' can be the basis for a common school district. This demonstrates a clear racial classification in the provision of education, which is a characteristic of Jim Crow laws. The confidence is high due to the explicit mention of race in the context of educational institutions. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' from the source title 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY 1870'.

classification.title: AN ACT to prohibit the importation, sale, or driving of Texas Cattle into certain Counties of this State, and fixing the period of the year when they may be brought to the other counties.
classification.summary: This act prohibits the importation, sale, or driving of Texas cattle into Kentucky except during the months of November, December, January, and February. It further prohibits the importation, bringing, or driving of these cattle into Bourbon, Clark, Montgomery, Mason, Fleming, Garrard, Lincoln, Boyle, or Franklin counties at any time of the year. Violators face penalties and potential arrest and bail. The act allows for the transportation of these cattle along public highways through exempted counties if en route to other counties, but only during the permitted months.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of cattle importation and movement within Kentucky. It establishes specific restrictions on the type of cattle (Texas cattle) and the counties and times they can be brought into the state. There is no mention of race or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'ACT' passed by the General Assembly and approved on a specific date. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clearly unrelated to racial segregation or discrimination.

classification.title: An Act to Regulate the Inspection and Sale of Oils
classification.summary: This act establishes regulations for the inspection and sale of illuminating oils, particularly those derived from coal, petroleum, or other bituminous substances. It sets a minimum fire-test temperature of 110° Fahrenheit, mandates branding for unsafe oils, and outlines forfeiture procedures for non-compliant products. The act also details the compensation for inspectors, penalties for fraud or neglect by inspectors, and the process for citizens to report suspected violations, leading to judicial warrants and testing of suspected articles. It further mandates the appointment of local inspectors by county judges and specifies the method for testing oils.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of oil quality and safety for illuminating purposes. It sets standards for oil testing, branding, and sale, along with penalties for violations and procedures for enforcement. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1870'.

It also allows the Commissioners to be made parties to any existing or future lawsuits involving the company where its constitutionality is questioned, and empowers them to employ counsel to protect the Commonwealth's and Sinking Fund's interests. The act is effective for two years.

classification.title: AN ACT to protect the Manufacturers and Bottlers of Mineral Water, Porter, Ale, Cider, and Lager Beer, and for other purposes.
classification.summary: This act establishes regulations for manufacturers and vendors of beverages like mineral water, porter, ale, cider, and lager beer. It requires them to file descriptions of their trade-marks (names or marks impressed on bottles) with the clerk of the district court in Louisville or Frankfort and publish these descriptions. The act makes it unlawful for anyone to refill or traffic in such marked bottles without the owner's permission, imposing penalties for violations. It also includes provisions for the compensation of the Commonwealth's Attorney and the collection of fines.

classification.title: RESOLUTION in relation to General Henry Crist.
classification.summary: This resolution directs the Auditor of the State to draw a warrant for twelve hundred and fifty dollars in favor of Muldoon, Bullitt & Co. for a monument erected over the remains of General Henry Crist. This action is taken because a previous joint resolution from March 9, 1868, directing the Governor to erect the monument, was not properly executed in accordance with constitutional provisions requiring a vote by yeas and nays on the journal of each house for every appropriation of money. The Auditor had initially declined to audit the warrant due to this procedural oversight.

No. 25 .
RESOLUTIONS in relation to a Water Communication between the Atlantic
and MississippiRiver.
WHEREAS , The Stateof Virginia and the Chesapeake and
Ohio Canal Company have resistedevery overture made to
them by foreigncapitalistsfor the surrender of theirfran-
chisesin the Kanawhaand James River Canal route,and have
thus given evidencethat itisthe wish of Virginia, the mag-
nanimous donator of the great Northwest Territory, to pro-
vide for that Territory a highway from the Ohio river to tide
water, that shallbe divested of allprivate interest,and as
near as possiblefreeto the whole nation; therefore,
Resolvedby the GeneralAssemblyof the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
1. That we look upon thischannel of communication,first
advocated by General George Washington ,between the valley
of the Mississippiand the Atlantic,as a work of nationalim-
portance,one involvingvitalinterestsnot only to the Western
States,but to thoseon the Western and Eastern slopeof the
Alleghanies- a promoter of the welfareof all.
2. That saidlineof water communication by the Kanawha
and James river,theirhead waters being but twenty-eight
and a half miles apart, is entitledto receive the careful
considerationof Congress,to the end that,being a work of
nationalimportance,it may receivesuch aid from the Gen-
eralGovernment ,under itsauthorityto regulatecommerce
between the States,as willsecuro itsearlycompletion.
3. That we recommend that the work of construction and
operationbe placed by Congress in the charge of nine trus-
tees,one of whom shallbe the Secretaryof the Interior,four
appointedby the Presidentof the United States,by and with
the advice and consent of the Senate,and one each in like
manner by the Statesof Virginia,West Virginia, Ohio ,and
Kentucky .
4.That theGovernorofKentuckyisherebyrequestedtoforward copiesof the foregoingresolutionsto the President
of the United States,with the requestthatthe same shallbe
by him laid beforeCongress; that copiesbe alsoforwarded
to the Governors of the severalStates,with the requestthat
they be laid before their respectiveLegislatures; and that
our Senators and Representativesin Congress be requested
by the Governor to use their best endeavors to secure the
passage of such measures as willconduce to the earlycon-
struction of the work .
Approved March 21, 1870.
1870 .

There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices targeting any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. natic Asylum at Lexington ; and whereas , the county
courtof saidcountyhas levieda tax upon the peopleof
said county,which has been collectedand paid to the

James Rowe ,afterhe was found to be a lunatic,two years
and six months - fivehundred and seventy-fivedollarsand
fiftycents .
Be itenactedby the General Assemblyof the Commonwealth

fivehundred and seventy-fivedollarsand fiftycents,in
favorof M. O. Allen,thepresidingjudge of Cumberland
county,tobe by him appropriatedto the payment of the
debt ofthecountyincurredforpublicbuildings,or toward

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of Cumberland County
classification.summary: This act instructs the Auditor of Public Accounts to draw a warrant for $575.50 from the public treasury to M. O. Allen, the presiding judge of Cumberland County. This money is to be used to pay a debt incurred by the county for public buildings or to complete the courthouse. The debt was incurred for the boarding and care of James Rowe, a man of color, who was found to be a lunatic and a pauper, and who was confined in jail due to his dangerous disposition after being refused admittance to the Western Lunatic Asylum and before being transferred to the Eastern Lunatic Asylum.

While race is mentioned, it does not appear to be the basis for any discriminatory practice or segregation, which are hallmarks of Jim Crow laws. The statute is focused on a specific financial matter related to an individual's care and county infrastructure. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Authorize the Town of [Town Name] to Issue Bonds and Levy Taxes
classification.summary: This act authorizes a town to issue bonds for town indebtedness and improvements, with interest at six percent per annum. It also allows the town board to levy an ad valorem tax and a poll tax to pay the interest and principal of these bonds. The marshal is responsible for collecting these taxes. The funds raised are to be applied to bond interest and principal payments, with any surplus to be invested. The act takes effect upon passage.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of the Elkhorn Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This act directs the Attorney General to initiate proceedings to forfeit the charter of the Elkhorn Turnpike Road Company. If forfeited, the court is to order a sale of the company's rights, franchises, and property. The purchaser must post bond to resume and complete the road within specified timeframes, with a potential change in route. If another turnpike company purchases the assets, it retains its original name.

classification.title: AN ACT to alter and amend the Road Law in Livingston County.
classification.summary: This act repeals the office of superintendent of roads in Livingston County and authorizes the county court to appoint two commissioners for each voting district. These commissioners will divide public roads into sections, let them out to the lowest bidder, and manage road work based on public advertisement, not exceeding the amount of road taxes collected in their districts.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Orphans' Home of the Baptist Churches of Louisville
classification.summary: This act incorporates an Orphans' Home for destitute children of Baptist parents and other helpless children. The institution is to be managed by a board of male managers selected from lay members of 'Regular United Baptists' churches in Louisville, composed of white persons. The managers are elected based on church contributions, with specific provisions for the president's selection and filling of vacancies. Pastors of self-sustaining churches are ex-officio members without voting rights.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that the managers are to be selected from 'lay members of the regularly organized Baptist churches of the city of Louisville, Kentucky, composed of white persons'. This explicit racial exclusion in the governance of an institution for children, even if not directly about segregation of services, indicates a racially discriminatory intent in its formation and operation. While the primary purpose is charitable, the explicit racial requirement for leadership makes it relevant to the Jim Crow era context, though not a direct segregation law. However, the prompt asks to classify statutes for a Jim Crow laws archive, and this statute's explicit racial exclusion in its foundational text warrants its inclusion as 'no' for being a direct Jim Crow law but with a racial component. The confidence is high because the racial exclusion is explicit. The category is 'education' as it pertains to the education of children. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the legislature. The racial indicator is 'explicit' due to the mention of 'white persons'.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend and reduce into one the several acts in relation to the Town of Crab Orchard, in Lincoln County.
classification.summary: This act amends and consolidates previous laws concerning the Town of Crab Orchard. It redefines the town's corporate limits, vests its governance in five trustees (three constituting a quorum), and establishes the 'Board of Trustees of the Town of Crab Orchard' as a corporate body. It also specifies that trustees are elected annually by free white male inhabitants over twenty-one who have paid their poll tax and any arrears.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'free white male inhabitants' as the electorate for town trustees. While this is a racial classification, it is a common feature of 19th-century voting laws and does not, in itself, constitute a Jim Crow law which typically refers to post-Reconstruction era statutes designed to disenfranchise Black Americans and enforce segregation. This statute is about local governance and voter eligibility based on race and property, which was prevalent at the time but not specifically a Jim Crow measure in the context of segregation or disenfranchisement of newly freed slaves. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, but the racial indicator is 'explicit' due to the mention of 'free white male inhabitants'.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Crab Orchard
classification.summary: This act details the governance of the town of Crab Orchard, Kentucky, by a board of trustees. It outlines the qualifications for voting in trustee elections (real estate ownership and residency), the term of office for trustees, their oath of office, and their powers to fill vacancies, appoint a chairman, and conduct elections. It also specifies notice requirements for elections and procedures for declaring a trustee's seat vacant due to absence. The act further grants trustees the power to set meeting times and places, impose penalties for non-attendance, and manage town property including streets and alleys, with the authority to take legal action for trespass or property recovery.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the administrative and governance structure of the town of Crab Orchard, Kentucky. It details the election and duties of town trustees, including voting qualifications, term limits, and the management of town property. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Stanford
classification.summary: This act grants the board of trustees of the town of Stanford the power to clear streets, alleys, sidewalks, and passageways of obstructions, keep public grounds free from encumbrances, remove nuisances, regulate combustible materials, ensure chimneys and fireplaces are safe, and require property owners to keep the areas in front of their properties clean. It also allows trustees to ornament the town with fencing, trees, and shrubs. Enforcement mechanisms include recovery of costs through warrants or suits.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the powers and responsibilities of a town's board of trustees regarding public spaces, sanitation, and beautification. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The language and scope of the statute are purely administrative and related to municipal governance. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The act focuses on the internal governance and taxation of the town.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the city of Henderson.
classification.summary: This act details the administrative and financial duties of the treasurer and clerk appointed by the board of trustees of the city of Henderson. It outlines oath requirements, bond specifications, receipt and payment procedures for funds, record-keeping, and reporting obligations. The treasurer is authorized to receive taxes paid by July 1st annually and must report all received and unpaid taxes by that date. Compensation for the treasurer is capped at 2.5% of moneys handled. The clerk also has oath and bond requirements and specific duties related to resolutions and records.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text focuses on the administrative and financial responsibilities of city officials (treasurer and clerk) within the city of Henderson. It details procedures for handling funds, record-keeping, and tax collection. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Stanford
classification.summary: This act details the duties and appointment of town officials, specifically the clerk and the marshal. It outlines their responsibilities regarding record-keeping, financial management, tax collection, and the execution of legal processes within the town and county. The marshal is given powers similar to sheriffs for tax collection and is authorized to serve notices and execute warrants and processes from the police judge. The act also permits the marshal to appoint a deputy.

It also empowers the council to issue bonds up to $300,000 to finance these works, with bonds being exempt from municipal taxation. The act specifies how the bonds should be signed and allows for the employment of an engineer during construction.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the amendment of the City Charter of Covington to authorize the construction of water-works and the issuance of bonds. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the city of Covington
classification.summary: This act grants the city council of Covington the power to fix water rents, condemn land for waterworks, and appoint commissioners to manage the waterworks. It outlines the powers and responsibilities of these commissioners, including appointing a superintendent and making monthly reports.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administrative and operational aspects of the waterworks in the city of Covington. It focuses on the powers of the city council and appointed commissioners regarding water rates, land acquisition for infrastructure, and the management of the water system. There is no mention of race, racial segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to empower the Boyd County Court to make subscription to the Capital Stock of the Ashland and Catlettsburg Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Boyd County Court, with a majority of justices concurring, to subscribe up to five thousand dollars to the capital stock of the Ashland and Catlettsburg Turnpike Road Company. The cost of a bridge construction near McGee's is to be included as part of this stock. The county court is also empowered to levy an ad valorem tax not exceeding twenty-five cents per one hundred dollars on taxable property in the county to fund this subscription, with the tax collectible by the sheriff like state revenue.
keywords: Ashland and Catlettsburg Turnpike Road Company
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the authorization for the Boyd County Court to invest in a turnpike road company and levy taxes to support this investment. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT in relation to hauling on the Turnpike Roads in Bourbon County belonging to Turnpike Road Companies in which the Bourbon County Court is the owner of Stock.
classification.summary: This act prohibits hauling more than six thousand pounds of freight (exclusive of wagon weight) on specific turnpike roads in Bourbon County. Violators face fines between ten and twenty dollars for each offense, payable to the turnpike road company.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute, Chapter 221 of the 1870 Laws of Kentucky, regulates the weight of freight that can be hauled on certain turnpike roads in Bourbon County. It sets a maximum load limit and specifies penalties for violations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. Confidence is high as the content is clear and unambiguous regarding its lack of racial component.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Versailles and Crawfish Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Versailles and Crawfish Turnpike Road Company, outlining the process for subscribing to its stock, electing its officers, and defining its corporate powers. It specifies the amount of stock per share, the commissioners responsible for opening subscription books, and the procedures for calling meetings and electing a president and directors. The company is granted perpetual succession and the rights and privileges of a body politic and corporate.
classification.reasoning: The statute clearly describes the incorporation of a private company for building a turnpike road. It details the financial and organizational aspects of the company, such as stock subscriptions, commissioner appointments, and the election of officers. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices that would classify it as a Jim Crow law. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law, and the confidence is high.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act,entitled " An act to regulate the sale of liquors," approved March 2d, 1860.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law regarding the sale of liquors in Louisville. It prohibits the sale of common, spruce, or lager beer, or mixtures thereof, to any white person under the age of eighteen years without the written consent of the minor's father, or mother or guardian if the father is deceased. A penalty of fifty dollars is imposed for each offense.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white person' in relation to the sale of alcohol to minors. While it does not establish a separate or unequal system for Black individuals, the explicit racial classification in a law regulating behavior and imposing penalties indicates a racial dimension. However, it does not fit the typical definition of Jim Crow laws which focused on segregation and disenfranchisement. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, but the explicit racial indicator is noted. The confidence is high because the text is clear, but human review might be beneficial to confirm the broader context of this specific regulation within the historical period.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Louisville, Cincinnati, and Lexington Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act consolidates the Louisville and Frankfort Railroad Company and the Lexington and Frankfort Railroad Company into a new entity, the Louisville, Cincinnati, and Lexington Railroad Company. It specifies the capital stock, share values, and the process for issuing new stock certificates. It also outlines the management structure of the new company, including the board of directors and president, and sets qualifications for directors, such as stock ownership and residency requirements. The act transfers all rights, franchises, property, and liabilities from the old companies to the new one.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the consolidation of two railroad companies and the management of the new entity. It does not contain any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to establish a Work-house in Christian County," approved January 28, 1870.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law establishing a workhouse in Christian County. It allows juries in Christian County prosecutions, where the punishment is confinement in the county jail, to instead specify confinement in the county workhouse. Peace officers are then required to deliver arrested persons to the workhouse keeper instead of the jailer.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the establishment and operation of a workhouse in Christian County, Kentucky. It dictates how convicted individuals are to be confined, allowing for workhouse confinement instead of jail. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clearly about penal administration and lacks any racial component.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the town of Hopkinsville, and to establish a work-house in Christian county.
classification.summary: This act establishes a work-house in Christian County, Kentucky. It mandates that individuals convicted of vagrancy in Christian County shall be confined in this work-house instead of the county jail. The court can adjudge confinement for a period not exceeding six months. The county court is authorized to make confined persons labor on streets and public highways, and to appoint a person to keep the work-house and employ guards. This provision takes effect upon the erection of the work-house.
classification.reasoning: The statute establishes a work-house in Christian County and mandates that individuals convicted of vagrancy be confined there and made to perform labor on public works. While the statute does not explicitly mention race, vagrancy laws were historically used to control and exploit Black populations after the Civil War. The broad language regarding 'persons convicted of vagrancy' and the requirement for forced labor strongly suggest an implicit racial dimension, as such laws were often disproportionately applied to Black individuals. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law with implicit racial indicators. The confidence is moderate because the racial aspect is inferred from historical context rather than explicit text. Human review is recommended to confirm the historical application of this law.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Paris and Tennessee River Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Paris and Tennessee River Railroad Company, outlining its powers, the route of its main line and branches, and appointing commissioners to receive subscriptions to its capital stock. The company is empowered to contract, sue and be sued, hold property, and construct a railway. The main line is to run from Paris through Winchester and Richmond towards the Tennessee line, with branches to industrial sites. The capital stock is limited to forty thousand dollars per lineal mile.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act of incorporation for a railroad company. It details the company's legal powers, the proposed route of its railway, and the process for raising capital through stock subscriptions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It details the process for stock sales, union of companies, and the extension of the charter over acquired roads. The act also specifies which cities, counties, and towns are eligible to participate in aiding the construction of the road and its branches.

The confidence is high because the content is clearly unrelated to racial discrimination.

classification.title: AN ACT to change the Road Law in Kenton County
classification.summary: This act mandates that the clerk of the Kenton county court include the full amount of road tax in tax bills, including a sum for each tithe. The sheriff is responsible for collecting this tax unless paid in labor. Individuals assessed with road tax can pay in labor at a rate of one dollar per day, provided the labor is performed during the first week of September. The surveyor's receipt for labor will be accepted by the sheriff as payment.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns road taxes and the method of payment in Kenton County. It outlines the duties of the county clerk and sheriff regarding tax collection and allows for payment in labor. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It details the appointment and election of board members, their corporate powers, control over school funds, appointment of teachers, regulation of discipline and curriculum, and the use of tax revenue for school purposes. The act also specifies that no person embraced by the common school laws of the state shall be excluded from the benefits of these schools.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the establishment and governance of a school district in Ashland, Kentucky. It outlines the powers and duties of the board of education, including the appointment of teachers and the regulation of school matters. While it mentions that no person embraced by the common school laws of the state shall be excluded, there is no explicit or implicit mention of racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is on the administrative and operational aspects of the school district. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act amends previous legislation incorporating the City of Maysville. It modifies the process for tax assessment and collection, declaring taxes due on or before June 1st annually and instructing collectors to proceed with collection without delay.

classification.title: An Act to Amend an Act Entitled "An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Louisville," Approved March 9, 1854, and to Amend Section 5 of Said Act.
classification.summary: This statute details the duties and responsibilities of a city tax collector, including the collection of taxes, the application of discounts for early payment, penalties for late payment, and reporting requirements to the city council. It also outlines the process for the city council to credit the collector for errors, deductions, and delinquents.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text focuses entirely on the administrative and financial procedures for a city tax collector. There is no mention of race, and the regulations described are standard for municipal tax collection and do not appear to have any discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of Stephen Howard, late Sheriff of Magoffin County, and his sureties.
classification.summary: This act provides relief to Stephen Howard, former Sheriff of Magoffin County, and his sureties. It allows them to be released from damages on judgments for revenue due in 1861 and 1862 if the principal, interest, and costs (excluding attorney's fees) are paid by May 1st of the current year. The act also includes a separate section for Milo Baxter and S.C. Powell, Trustees of School District No. 60 in Madison County, allowing for a delayed report for the year 1868.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns financial relief for a former sheriff and his sureties regarding unpaid revenue and damages. It also addresses a procedural matter for school trustees. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Town of Walton
classification.summary: This act incorporates the town of Walton, granting its town council powers to provide for the punishment of offenders against town ordinances by imprisonment, publish annual financial statements, and manage town affairs. There is no mention of race, and the provisions appear to be general in nature, applying to all residents of the town. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.
