classification.title: An Act to provide for the subscription of stock in, or loan of money to, companies organized for the construction of railroads, turnpikes, or other works of internal improvement.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process by which counties, towns, or cities can vote to subscribe to the capital stock of, or make loans to, companies involved in internal improvements such as railroads or turnpikes. It details the notice requirements for such elections, the appointment of election officers, the conduct of the election, and the recording of the results. If a majority of voters favor the proposition, the subscription or loan is to be made, and bonds will be executed on behalf of the municipality. The statute also specifies limits on the amount that can be subscribed or loaned relative to the assessed property value.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the process for local governments to invest in internal improvement projects through public votes and bond issuance. It does not contain any language, explicit or implicit, that suggests racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1870.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Madisonville and Shawneetown Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act grants powers to the president and directors of the Madisonville and Shawneetown Railroad Company. These powers include appointing and removing officers, determining their pay, regulating accounts and employee liability, erecting buildings, transferring stock, and passing by-laws. It also allows for increasing capital stock and grants broad authority for the construction of the railroad, including land acquisition, contracting, and material use. The act specifies that bridges must not obstruct navigation and allows for agreements with landowners for materials and land use.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the incorporation and operational powers of a railroad company. It outlines the company's ability to appoint officers, manage finances, construct infrastructure, and acquire land. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1870'.

classification.title: Proceedings if cannot agree
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for land acquisition by companies when agreement with the property owner cannot be reached. It details the appointment of a justice of the peace, the summoning of a jury of twenty discreet men, the process of striking jurors, the oath administered to the jury, and how damages are to be estimated. The jury is to consider the actual value of the property, necessary fencing and ditching, and offset any advantages to the remaining property from the company's operations. The verdict is to be reduced to writing, signed, returned to the county court clerk, confirmed, and recorded. If set aside, another inquisition is to be held. The statute specifies that the valuation, when tendered or paid, entitles the company to the land or interest.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the legal procedures for eminent domain, specifically how companies can acquire property when they cannot reach an agreement with the owner. The text focuses on the process of valuation, jury selection, and compensation. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 1870 .' and the content being an act passed during that legislative session.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Madisonville and Shawneetown Straight Line Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act details the process by which counties, cities, and towns can subscribe to the capital stock of the Madisonville and Shawneetown Straight Line Railroad Company. It outlines procedures for submitting the question to qualified voters within designated precincts, cities, or towns, and specifies how the vote should be conducted, recorded, and acted upon. If a majority favors the subscription, the relevant governing body is authorized to issue bonds to finance the stock purchase.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the incorporation and financing of a railroad company. It outlines the process for local governments to vote on subscribing to the company's stock. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the town of Pitt's Point, in Livingston county.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of Pitt's Point, Livingston County. It grants the board of trustees powers to regulate licenses, tax shows, and pass ordinances for the town's good government. It also legalizes past acts of the board, including property conveyances for unpaid taxes. Notably, it extends the incorporation to include lots in the Carrico addition, specifically mentioning a lot East of Frank Bowman's (colored). It also establishes a lien for town taxes and exempts a specific riverbank and ferry landing from town taxes.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'Frank Bowman's (colored)' when defining the extended incorporation limits. While this is an explicit racial mention, the primary purpose of the act is to amend a town's charter and grant it governmental powers, not to establish a discriminatory law in the vein of typical Jim Crow statutes like segregation or disenfranchisement. The mention of 'colored' appears to be descriptive of a property owner rather than a basis for discriminatory legislation within this specific act. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law, but the racial indicator is 'explicit' due to the direct mention of race.

classification.title: An Act to authorize the Commissioners of the Sinking Fund for Hardin County to sell bonds and stock.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Commissioners of the Sinking Fund for Hardin County to issue and sell bonds, not less than one hundred dollars and not more than one thousand dollars each, with interest not exceeding seven percent per annum, payable semi-annually. It also allows them to receive money from citizens or corporations for redeeming existing county bonds, issuing certificates for repayment with interest. Furthermore, the act permits the sale of a portion of the county's stock in the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company, at a rate not less than seventy dollars per share, to pay off county debts. All moneys received and dividends are to be managed by the commissioners or their treasurer.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the financial management of Hardin County, specifically authorizing the issuance and sale of bonds, the redemption of existing debt through advances from citizens, and the sale of county stock in a railroad company. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it appears to be an act passed by the legislature in a specific year (1870).

classification.title: An Act to amend an act, entitled 'An act to charter the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company'
classification.summary: This act concerns the financial management and bond redemption for the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company, specifically for Hardin County and later extended to Hart, Warren, and Logan Counties. It details how funds from stock sales and other revenue sources are to be used for debt repayment and outlines the process for issuing new bonds. It also grants bondholders similar enforcement rights as existing bondholders and specifies conditions for bond issuance and payment arrangements with the railroad company.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided deals exclusively with the financial obligations and management of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company and its associated counties. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices that would classify it as a Jim Crow law. The focus is purely on fiscal matters, bond issuance, and repayment mechanisms. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law, and the confidence is high.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Bank of America
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Bank of America in Louisville, Kentucky, establishing its capital, corporate powers, board of directors, and operational procedures. It outlines the election of directors, their powers to receive subscriptions, declare dividends, appoint officers, and manage the bank's business. The stock is defined as personal property and assignable, with the corporation holding a lien on stock for indebtedness.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute is an act to incorporate a bank. It details the establishment, governance, and operational aspects of the Bank of America. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. Confidence is high as the content is clearly unrelated to Jim Crow legislation.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Louisville Banking Company
classification.summary: This act establishes the Louisville Banking Company, outlining its capital stock, election of directors, payment procedures for shares, commencement of business, and powers related to deposits, loans, and real estate. It details the process for opening books, receiving subscriptions, electing a board, and managing forfeited stock. The bank is authorized to receive various forms of deposits, engage in lending, deal in exchange and debt instruments, and acquire property.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the incorporation and operational procedures of a banking company. It outlines financial regulations, the election of directors, and the bank's powers regarding deposits and loans. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an act passed by the General Assembly in 1870.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporatethe MayfieldCreek Navigating Company ,in Ballard County .
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Mayfield Creek Navigating Company in Ballard County. It aims to improve navigation on Mayfield Creek by removing obstructions that cause flooding and sickness, thereby enabling agricultural development and commerce for local farmers. The company is granted powers to manage the creek and its surrounding lands.
classification.reasoning: The statute incorporates a company to improve navigation on Mayfield Creek. The preamble discusses the benefits of removing obstructions, such as preventing floods, improving sanitation, and facilitating agriculture and commerce. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act outlines the powers and responsibilities of town trustees regarding the management of streets, alleys, and public spaces. It details their authority to levy taxes, maintain infrastructure, make by-laws, and employ legal counsel. It also specifies the qualifications for town officers, including a police judge, town marshal, and trustees, requiring them to be white males over twenty-one years old. The act also defines the jurisdiction of the police judge and the process for handling civil and criminal cases within the town.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that town officers, including the police judge, town marshal, and trustees, must be 'white males over twenty-one years old.' This is a clear racial qualification for holding public office, which is a characteristic of Jim Crow era legislation, even though the rest of the statute deals with municipal governance. Therefore, it is classified as 'yes' for Jim Crow. The confidence is high because the racial requirement is explicit. The category is null because the primary focus of the statute is municipal governance and not a specific area like education or public accommodations, although the racial qualification is a form of discriminatory practice. It outlines their responsibilities regarding the execution and return of legal processes, the collection of fines and taxes, and their accountability. The act also specifies the commissioning of the police judge by the Governor and the election of the town marshal.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administrative and judicial structure of the town of Clayville, specifically concerning the roles and responsibilities of a police judge and a town marshal. It outlines procedures for legal processes, the collection of town revenue, and the filling of vacancies. There is no mention of race, racial segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it appears to be an act passed by the legislature in 1870. Confidence is high because the content is clearly administrative and lacks any racial component.

classification.title: AN ACT further to amend the Charter of the Glasgow Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Glasgow Railroad Company by directing the county court judge of Barren County to levy a tax on taxable property in precinct number one to pay interest on bonds issued to the company and cover assessment and collection expenses. It also outlines allowances for the assessor and sheriff, and specifies how future levies for bond interest and sinking funds will be made based on prior assessments. Additionally, it details payment for the assessor's designation of citizens and property locations within the precinct and appoints three individuals to supervise the assessment.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial and administrative aspects of a railroad company's charter, specifically regarding taxation, bond payments, and assessment procedures within a particular county precinct. There is no mention of race, and the provisions do not appear to have any discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the Glasgow Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act grants the Glasgow Railroad Company the authority to extend its railroad line from Glasgow to the Tennessee State line. It allows the company to increase its capital stock, grants it rights and privileges for the extension including the right of way, and permits it to receive stock subscriptions from individuals, counties, or districts. The act outlines the process for county courts to submit subscription proposals to voters and to issue bonds and levy taxes to repay them if approved.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the expansion of the Glasgow Railroad Company, including provisions for funding through stock subscriptions and bond issuance, and the process for voter approval of such measures. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act addresses a specific incident involving a horse thief named Albert Greer. It details his capture, resistance, trial by justices of the peace Geo. H. Towery and J. M. It outlines the judge's role as a conservator of the peace, authority to order arrests (with or without warrants for offenses in his presence), and the ability to detain offenders. Defendants are given the right to replevy judgments.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY, 1870 - Cemeteries and Town Regulations
classification.summary: This section of the 1870 Laws of Kentucky outlines various powers granted to town boards of trustees. These include the authority to purchase and manage cemeteries, appoint inspectors, provide water, establish public pounds, regulate locomotives and railroads, control public scales, repair streets and infrastructure, establish hospitals and pest-houses, and enact regulations for health and the suppression of disease. It also includes a provision to prohibit work on the Sabbath day, with exceptions for household necessities, necessity, or charity, and exemptions for those observing a different day as their Sabbath.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Princeton, and to Provide for the Taxation and Working of Streets Therein
classification.summary: This act details the taxation of property within Princeton, including specific provisions for dwellings on farms and businesses. It also outlines exemptions from taxation for public property, educational institutions, churches, cemeteries, and household furniture up to a certain value. A significant portion of the act concerns the regulation of street work, requiring able-bodied male inhabitants between 16 and 50 (excluding ministers) to labor for three days annually on town streets, with an option to pay a fee instead. Failure to comply results in penalties.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Princeton
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of Princeton, Kentucky. It specifies that members of the board of trustees will not receive pay for their services. It also outlines the board's duty to maintain streets and alleys, with penalties for failure. Existing ordinances not inconsistent with the act remain in force. The act vests all actions, rights, fines, penalties, and forfeitures in the board of trustees and transfers all town property to them. It also clarifies that the act does not invalidate prior legal acts or rights and repeals inconsistent acts. The act takes effect twenty days after its passage.

said road towards the court-house in said city,to the
linebetween the lotsof Matt . Turney and H. M. Rucker ;
thence with said line, between Turney and Rucker , to
the western bank of Houston creek; thence along said
bank of said creek, with its meanders, to the present
boundary ,be ,and the same is hereby ,excluded from the
corporatelimitsof saidcity,and shallnot hereaftercon-
stitutea part thereof; and said line herein described
shall,as far as it goes, constitutethe boundary of said
city.
§ 3. This act shall take effectand be in force from its
passage .
Approved February 28, 1870.
